# Remult

> Remult is a TypeScript library for building full-stack apps without boilerplate. Define entities once and use them on the frontend, the backend, and in the database, with a single SSOT (Single Source of Truth) for schema, validation, permissions, and CRUD.

## Core Concepts

- **Entity** - a TypeScript class decorated with `@Entity('key', options)` is your SSOT: schema, REST API, validation, permissions, and lifecycle in one place.
- **Fields** - `@Fields.string()`, `.number()`, `.boolean()`, `.date()`, `.json()`, `.uuid()`, `.cuid()`, `.autoIncrement()`, plus `@Relations.toOne()` / `.toMany()` for entity links.
- **Repository (`repo(Entity)`)** - universal CRUD: `.find()`, `.findFirst()`, `.findId()`, `.insert()`, `.update()`, `.delete()`, `.save()`, `.count()`, `.query()`, `.liveQuery()`. Same API on frontend and backend.
- **Permissions** - entity-level `allowApiRead/Insert/Update/Delete/Crud` and per-field `allowApiUpdate`; values are boolean, role string, role array, or `(entity, remult) => boolean`. Use `apiPrefilter` / `backendPrefilter` for row-level filtering.
- **Lifecycle hooks** - `validation`, `saving`, `saved`, `deleting`, `deleted` run on the server before/after persistence; `saving` mutates the entity in place.
- **BackendMethod** - `@BackendMethod({ allowed })` exposes a server-only function callable from the client; runs in the server context with `remult.user` populated.
- **Live queries** - `repo(X).liveQuery(options).subscribe(({ items, changes }) => ...)` pushes inserts/updates/deletes to all connected clients over SSE.
- **Custom filters** - `Filter.createCustom` / `SqlDatabase.filterToRaw` to express complex queries that round-trip to the server safely.
- **Validators** - built-ins under `Validators.*` (`required`, `min`, `max`, `email`, `url`, `unique`, `regex`, `enum`, ...) or `validate: (entity) => string | undefined` for custom logic.
- **Admin UI** - turnkey `/api/admin` panel via `admin: true` (or `admin: { allow: 'admin' }`) in `remultApi()`.
- **Stacks** - frameworks: React, Angular, Vue, SvelteKit, Next.js, SolidStart, Nuxt. Servers: Express, Fastify, Hono, Elysia, Hapi, Koa, NestJS. Databases: PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB, SQLite (better/sqljs/bun), MSSQL, Turso, DuckDB, Oracle, Cloudflare D1, JSON files.

## Documentation

### Getting Started
- [Introduction](https://remult.dev/docs/): With Remult it is simple to keep your code DRY and increase development speed and maintainability by defining a single TypeScript model class (for each domain object) and sharing it between your frontend and backend code.
- [Creating a Remult Project](https://remult.dev/docs/creating-a-project): The easiest way to start building a Remult app
- [Quickstart](https://remult.dev/docs/quickstart): Jumpstart your development with this Quickstart guide.
- [Example Apps](https://remult.dev/docs/example-apps): We have already a ton of examples!

### Entities
- [Entities](https://remult.dev/docs/entities): Entities are THE key concept of Remult - your Single Source of Truth (SSoT) for data structure, api, validation, permissions, and more!
- [Field Types](https://remult.dev/docs/field-types): There are also several built in Field decorators for common use case:
- [Relations Between Entities](https://remult.dev/docs/entity-relations): In Remult, entity relations play a useful role in modeling and navigating the complex relationships that exist within your data.
- [Filtering and Relations](https://remult.dev/docs/filtering-and-relations): In this article, we'll discuss several relevant techniques for one-to-many relations.
- [Entity Lifecycle Hooks](https://remult.dev/docs/lifecycle-hooks): In Remult, you can take advantage of Entity Lifecycle Hooks to add custom logic and actions at specific stages of an entity's lifecycle.
- [Migrations](https://remult.dev/docs/migrations): Managing database schemas is crucial in web development.
- [Generate Entities from Existing Database](https://remult.dev/docs/entities-codegen-from-db-schema): Want to use Remult for full-stack CRUD with your existing database?
- [Offline Support](https://remult.dev/docs/offline-support): In modern web applications, providing a seamless user experience often involves enabling offline functionality.
- [Mutability and the Active Record Pattern](https://remult.dev/docs/active-record): The Active Record pattern is a concept in software architecture, particularly useful when working with mutable objects whose state may change over time.
- [Entity Instance Backend Methods](https://remult.dev/docs/entity-backend-methods): When leveraging the Active Record pattern, backend methods for entity instances offer a powerful way to integrate client-side behavior with server-side logic.
- [Introduction to Mutable Controllers and Backend Methods](https://remult.dev/docs/mutable-controllers): In web development architectures, mutable controllers offer a convenient way to manage state and facilitate interactions between the client (frontend) and the server (backend).

### Stacks
- [Stacks](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/)
- [Select a framework](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/framework/)
- [React](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/framework/react): To enable the use of decorators in your React app, modify the vite.config.ts file by adding the following to the defineConfig section:
- [Angular](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/framework/angular): In development, your Angular app will be served from http://localhost:4200, while the API server will run on http://localhost:3002.
- [Vue](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/framework/vue): To enable the use of decorators in your React app, modify the vite.config.ts file by adding the following to the defineConfig section:
- [SvelteKit](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/framework/sveltekit): Install Remult and any necessary dependencies by running:
- [Next.js](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/framework/nextjs): Remult is bootstrapped in a Next.js app by creating a catch-all dynamic API route.
- [SolidStart](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/framework/solid): Once completed, navigate to the project directory:
- [Nuxt](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/framework/nuxt): To enable the use of TypeScript decorators in your Nuxt project, modify the nuxt.config.ts file as follows:
- [Select a server](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/server/)
- [Express](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/server/express): Remult is integrated into your backend as an Express middleware.
- [Fastify](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/server/fastify): Remult is integrated into your backend as Fastify middleware.
- [Hono](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/server/hono): Remult is integrated into your backend using the remultApi adapter for Hono.
- [Elysia](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/server/elysia): Remult is integrated into your backend using the remultApi adapter for Hono
- [Hapi](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/server/hapi): Remult is integrated into your backend as a Hapi plugin.
- [Koa](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/server/koa): Remult is integrated into your backend as middleware for Koa.
- [Nest.js](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/server/nest): Your Nest.js app with Remult is now up and running on port 3002.
- [Choose a Database](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/database/): By default, if no database provider is specified, Remult will use a simple JSON file-based database.
- [PostgreSQL](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/database/postgresql): To set up PostgreSQL as the database provider for your Remult application, you'll need to configure the dataProvider property in the api.ts file.
- [MySQL](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/database/mysql)
- [MongoDB](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/database/mongodb): This setup connects to a MongoDB instance running on localhost and uses the test database.
- [SQLite3](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/database/sqlite3): This version of SQLite3 works well even on platforms like StackBlitz.
- [Better SQLite3](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/database/better-sqlite3): To use Better-sqlite3 as the database provider for your Remult application, follow these steps:
- [sqljs](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/database/sqljs): This configuration allows you to use an in-memory SQLite database in your Remult application, powered by sql.js.
- [Microsoft SQL Server](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/database/mssql): This setup lets you easily connect Remult to Microsoft SQL Server using knex for query building and tedious as the driver.
- [Bun SQLite](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/database/bun-sqlite): This setup allows you to use Bun's SQLite implementation as the database provider for your Remult application, leveraging Bun’s performance benefits with SQLite.
- [Turso](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/database/turso): This setup allows you to use Turso as the backend database for your application.
- [DuckDb](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/database/duckdb): This setup allows you to use DuckDB as your database provider in a Remult project.
- [Oracle](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/database/oracle): This setup integrates Oracle into your Remult-based application.
- [D1](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/database/d1)
- [Json files](https://remult.dev/docs/installation/database/json): This configuration allows you to store and manage your application data in JSON files, ideal for small projects or quick setups.

### Server-side Code
- [Backend Methods](https://remult.dev/docs/backendMethods): Backend methods run on the backend and are used to improve performance, execute server-only code (e.g., sending emails), or perform operations not accessible through the API.
- [Backend only code](https://remult.dev/docs/using-server-only-packages): One of the main advantages of remult is that you write code once, and it runs both on the server and in the browser.

### Guides
- [Access Control](https://remult.dev/docs/access-control): Access control is essential for ensuring that users can only access resources they are authorized to in web applications.
- [Admin UI](https://remult.dev/docs/admin-ui): Enjoy a fully featured Admin UI for your entities, you can do CRUD operations on your entities, view their relationships via the Diagram entry, and ensure secure management with the same validations and authorizations as your application.
- [Modules](https://remult.dev/docs/modules): The module system arrived in remult@3.0.6, the idea is to pack features in a single place.
- [Community modules](https://remult.dev/docs/modules-community): This page showcases a list of recommended community made modules.

### Escape Hatches
- [Leveraging Custom Filters for Enhanced Data Filtering](https://remult.dev/docs/custom-filter): In modern web applications, efficiently filtering data is essential for providing a seamless user experience.
- [Accessing the Underlying Database in Remult](https://remult.dev/docs/running-sql-on-the-server): While Remult provides a powerful abstraction for working with databases, there might be scenarios where you need to access the underlying database directly.
- [Using Remult in Non-Remult Routes](https://remult.dev/docs/using-remult-in-custom-backend-code): When using the CRUD api or BackendMethods, remult is automatically available.
- [Working without decorators](https://remult.dev/docs/working-without-decorators): If you prefer to work without decorators, or use remult in a javascript project (without typescript) you can use the following:
- [Extensibility](https://remult.dev/docs/custom-options): Module Augmentation in TypeScript allows you to extend existing types with custom properties or methods.

### Integrations
- [Adding Swagger and openApi](https://remult.dev/docs/adding-swagger): In short, swagger provides a quick UI that describes the api which is exposed by the application.
- [Adding Graphql](https://remult.dev/docs/adding-graphql): In the /src/server/index.ts file add the following code:
- [Standard Schema](https://remult.dev/docs/standard-schema): Remult now supports the Standard Schema specification, allowing you to use your Remult entities as type-safe validators that are compatible with any Standard Schema library.
- [Docs for LLMs](https://remult.dev/docs/llms): We support the llms.txt convention for making documentation available to large language models and the agents that wrap them.

### API Reference
- [Entity](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_entity): Decorates classes that should be used as entities.
- [Field](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_field): Decorates fields that should be used as fields. for more info see: Field Types
- [ValueConverter](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_valueconverter): Interface for converting values between different formats, such as in-memory objects, database storage, JSON data transfer objects (DTOs), and HTML input elements.
- [Validation](https://remult.dev/docs/validation): Validation is a key part of any application, and you will see that it's builtin Remult !
- [Validators](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_validators): Class containing various field validators.
- [Relations](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_relations): Define a toMany relation between entities, indicating a one-to-many relationship.
- [RelationOptions](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_relationoptions): Options for configuring a relation between entities.
- [Remult](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_remult): Return's a Repository of the specific entity type
- [ApiClient](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_apiclient): Interface for configuring the API client used by Remult to perform HTTP calls to the backend.
- [Repository](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_repository): used to perform CRUD operations on an entityType
- [RemultServerOptions](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_remultserveroptions): Entities to use for the api
- [EntityFilter](https://remult.dev/docs/entityFilter): Used to filter the desired result set
- [EntityMetadata](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_entitymetadata): Metadata for an Entity, this metadata can be used in the user interface to provide a richer UI experience
- [FieldMetadata](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_fieldmetadata): Metadata for a Field, this metadata can be used in the user interface to provide a richer UI experience
- [Allowed](https://remult.dev/docs/allowed): Throughout the api you'll see methods that use the Allowed data type, for example allowApiRead etc...
- [BackendMethod](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_backendmethod): Decorator indicating that the decorated method runs on the backend.
- [QueryResult](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_queryresult): The result of a call to the query method in the Repository object.
- [Paginator](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_paginator): An interface used to paginating using the query method in the Repository object
- [LiveQuery](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_livequery): The LiveQuery interface represents a live query that allows subscribing to changes in the query results.
- [LiveQueryChangeInfo](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_livequerychangeinfo): The LiveQueryChangeInfo interface represents information about changes in the results of a live query.
- [Filter](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_filter): The Filter class is a helper class that focuses on filter-related concerns.
- [Sort](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_sort): The Sort class is used to describe sorting criteria for queries.
- [SqlDatabase](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_sqldatabase): A DataProvider for Sql Databases
- [SubscriptionChannel](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_subscriptionchannel): The SubscriptionChannel class is used to send messages from the backend to the frontend, using the same mechanism used by live queries.
- [generateMigrations](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_generatemigrations): Generates migration scripts based on changes in entities.
- [migrate](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_migrate): Applies migration scripts to update the database schema.
- [initAsyncHooks](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_initasynchooks): Initializes and enables async context tracking for the server.
- [Entity Rest Api Breakdown](https://remult.dev/docs/rest-api): All entities automatically expose a rest API based on the parameters defined in its decorator.
- [EntityBase](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_entitybase)
- [IdEntity](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_identity)
- [EntityRef](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_entityref)
- [FieldRef](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_fieldref): Loads the related value - returns null if the related value is not found
- [getEntityRef](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_getentityref): Retrieves the EntityRef object associated with the specified entity instance.
- [getFields](https://remult.dev/docs/ref_getfields)

## Tutorials

Each framework tutorial walks through the same 10 lessons (Setup, Entities, Paging/Sorting/Filtering, CRUD, Validation, Live Queries, Backend methods, Auth, Database, Deployment) with framework-specific code samples.

### React Tutorial (canonical)
- [Build a Full-Stack React Application](https://remult.dev/tutorials/react/): In this tutorial, we are going to create a simple app to manage a task list.
- [Entities](https://remult.dev/tutorials/react/entities): Let's start coding the app by defining the Task entity class.
- [Paging, Sorting and Filtering](https://remult.dev/tutorials/react/sorting-filtering): The RESTful API created by Remult supports server-side paging, sorting, and filtering.
- [CRUD Operations](https://remult.dev/tutorials/react/crud): Now that we can see the list of tasks, it's time to add a few more.
- [Validation](https://remult.dev/tutorials/react/validation): Validating user entered data is usually required both on the client-side and on the server-side, often causing a violation of the DRY design principle.
- [Live Queries](https://remult.dev/tutorials/react/live-queries): Our todo list app can have multiple users using it at the same time.
- [Backend methods](https://remult.dev/tutorials/react/backend-methods): When performing operations on multiple entity objects, performance considerations may necessitate running them on the server.
- [Authentication and Authorization](https://remult.dev/tutorials/react/auth): Our todo app is nearly functionally complete, but it still doesn't fulfill a very basic requirement - that users should log in before they can view, create or modify tasks.
- [Database](https://remult.dev/tutorials/react/database): Up until now the todo app has been using a plain JSON file to store the list of tasks.
- [Deployment](https://remult.dev/tutorials/react/deployment): Let's deploy the todo app to railway.app.

### Same tutorial, other frameworks
- [Angular Tutorial](https://remult.dev/tutorials/angular/): Same 10-lesson structure as the React tutorial above, with Angular-specific code samples.
- [Vue Tutorial](https://remult.dev/tutorials/vue/): Same 10-lesson structure as the React tutorial above, with Vue-specific code samples.
- [SvelteKit Tutorial](https://remult.dev/tutorials/sveltekit/): Same 10-lesson structure as the React tutorial above, with SvelteKit-specific code samples.
- [Next.js Tutorial](https://remult.dev/tutorials/react-next/): Same 10-lesson structure as the React tutorial above, with Next.js-specific code samples.
- [SolidStart Tutorial](https://remult.dev/tutorials/solid-start/): Same 10-lesson structure as the React tutorial above, with SolidStart-specific code samples.

## Interactive Tutorial
> Hands-on, browser-based tutorial at https://learn.remult.dev

### Basics
- [Introduction - Welcome to the Remult Tutorial](https://learn.remult.dev/1-basics/1-introduction/0-intro): Hey there, and welcome to the Remult Tutorial! 👋
- [Introduction - Entity](https://learn.remult.dev/1-basics/1-introduction/1-entity): In Remult, the core element is an entity.
- [Introduction - Rest Api](https://learn.remult.dev/1-basics/1-introduction/2-rest-api): For this tutorial, we'll use Express (Remult works with many JavaScript web frameworks including Express, Fastify, Next.js, Sveltekit, nuxt.js, Hapi, Hono, Nest, and Koa).
- [Introduction - Insert Data on the Backend](https://learn.remult.dev/1-basics/1-introduction/3-insert-data-on-the-backend): Next, we'll add some tasks on the backend so we can use them later.
- [Introduction - Display the Task List](https://learn.remult.dev/1-basics/1-introduction/4-display-the-task-list): Next, we'll use the tasks from the backend and display them in the frontend.
- [Querying Data - Paging](https://learn.remult.dev/1-basics/2-querying-data/1-paging): The RESTful API created by Remult supports server-side paging, sorting, and filtering.
- [Querying Data - Sorting](https://learn.remult.dev/1-basics/2-querying-data/2-sorting): Remult's RESTful API also supports server-side sorting.
- [Querying Data - Filtering](https://learn.remult.dev/1-basics/2-querying-data/3-filtering): Remult's RESTful API also supports server-side filtering.
- [Manipulating Data - Insert](https://learn.remult.dev/1-basics/3-manipulating-data/1-Insert): First, let's add the React code for adding a new task.
- [Manipulating Data - Update](https://learn.remult.dev/1-basics/3-manipulating-data/2-update): Let's add the functionality to update a task's completion status.
- [Manipulating Data - Delete](https://learn.remult.dev/1-basics/3-manipulating-data/3-delete): Let's add the functionality to delete a task.
- [Validation - Required](https://learn.remult.dev/1-basics/4-validation/1-required): In most web applications, validation code is spread and duplicated across multiple places.
- [Validation - Built-In Validations](https://learn.remult.dev/1-basics/4-validation/2-built-in-validations): Remult comes with a set of built-in validations that you can easily choose from.
- [Validation - Custom Validations](https://learn.remult.dev/1-basics/4-validation/3-custom-validations): You can also define custom validation logic for your fields.
- [Live Query - Live Query](https://learn.remult.dev/1-basics/5-live-query/1-introduction): Our todo list app can have multiple users using it at the same time.
- [Live Query - Realtime Updates](https://learn.remult.dev/1-basics/5-live-query/2-realtime-updates): To enable real-time updates, we'll modify the useEffect hook to use liveQuery.
- [Backend Methods - Updating Multiple Tasks](https://learn.remult.dev/1-basics/6-backend-methods/1-introduction): When performing operations on multiple entity objects, performance considerations may necessitate running them on the server.
- [Backend Methods - Refactor to Backend](https://learn.remult.dev/1-basics/6-backend-methods/2-refactor-to-backend): To improve performance, let's refactor the frontend code and move it to the backend.
- [Authentication & Authorization - Authentication and Authorization](https://learn.remult.dev/1-basics/7-auth/1-introduction): Our todo app is nearly functionally complete, but it still doesn't fulfill a very basic requirement - that users should log in before they can view, create, or modify tasks.
- [Authentication & Authorization - Authentication](https://learn.remult.dev/1-basics/7-auth/2-user-authentication): In this lesson, we'll implement a basic sign-in mechanism using cookie session.
- [Authentication & Authorization - Role-based Authorization](https://learn.remult.dev/1-basics/7-auth/3-authorization): In most applications, different users have different levels of access.
- [Authentication & Authorization - Role-based Authorization on the Frontend](https://learn.remult.dev/1-basics/7-auth/4-role-based-authorization-on-frontend): From a user experience perspective, it only makes sense that users who can't add or delete tasks shouldn't see the buttons for those actions.
- [Database - Database](https://learn.remult.dev/1-basics/8-Database/1-connection-to-a-database): Up until now the todo app has been using a plain JSON file to store the list of tasks.

### In Depth
- [Relations - Many to One](https://learn.remult.dev/4-concepts/1-relations/1-many-to-one): In this chapter, we’ll explore how to work with entity relations in Remult using customers and orders as our example.
- [Relations - One to Many](https://learn.remult.dev/4-concepts/1-relations/2-one-to-many): In this lesson, we'll explore how to set up and work with a one-to-many relation in Remult, where one Customer can have many Order records.
- [Relations - Id Based Relations](https://learn.remult.dev/4-concepts/1-relations/3-id-based-relations): ID-based relations provide more control over how related entities are managed.
- [Relations - Many to Many](https://learn.remult.dev/4-concepts/1-relations/4-many-to-many): In some applications, an entity might need to have a relationship with multiple entities from another table, and vice versa.
- [Advanced Filtering - Custom Filter](https://learn.remult.dev/4-concepts/4-filtering/1-custom-filters): In this lesson, we'll explore how to simplify and reuse filtering logic in your application using Custom Filters.
- [Advanced Filtering - Filter Based on Relation](https://learn.remult.dev/4-concepts/4-filtering/2-filter-based-on-relation): When working with relational data, you might encounter scenarios where you need to filter records based on data from related entities.
- [Advanced Filtering - Filter Based on Relation Using SQL](https://learn.remult.dev/4-concepts/4-filtering/3-fitler-based-on-relation-sql): In this lesson, we'll explore how to perform advanced filtering using SQL directly within custom filters.
- [Advanced Filtering - Sql Relations Filter](https://learn.remult.dev/4-concepts/4-filtering/4-sql-relations-filter): Filtering based on relations can be a powerful tool when querying data that involves multiple interconnected entities.
- [Sql Expression Entity Field - Introduction](https://learn.remult.dev/4-concepts/6-sql-expression/1-introduction): In Remult, sqlExpression fields provide a convenient way to bring SQL’s computational power directly into your entity fields.
- [Sql Expression Entity Field - Getting a field from a relation](https://learn.remult.dev/4-concepts/6-sql-expression/2-getting-a-field-from-a-relation): With Remult’s sqlExpression feature, you can create fields that pull data from related entities.
- [Sql Expression Entity Field - Sql Relations](https://learn.remult.dev/4-concepts/6-sql-expression/3-sql-relations): The sqlRelations API (currently experimental) enhances the way you handle relationships in Remult by enabling SQL-like expressions directly on related entities.
- [Access Control - Intro & Field-Level Authorization](https://learn.remult.dev/4-concepts/7-access-control/1-field-level-control): This lesson builds upon the foundational Authentication & Authorization article, so please review that if you haven't yet.
- [Access Control - Row-Level Authorization](https://learn.remult.dev/4-concepts/7-access-control/2-row-level-authorization): Row-level authorization enables control over specific rows of an entity based on user roles, ownership, or custom logic.
- [Access Control - Filtering Rows Based on User Permissions](https://learn.remult.dev/4-concepts/7-access-control/3-filtering-rows-based-on-User-Permissions): Filtering rows based on user permissions ensures that users only access the rows they are authorized to see, update, or delete.
- [Access Control - Filtering Related Rows Based on User Permissions](https://learn.remult.dev/4-concepts/7-access-control/4-filtering-related-rows-based-on-user-permissions): In this lesson, we’ll explore how to apply user-based access controls to related rows in your data model.
- [Access Control - Sql Relations Filter for User Permissions](https://learn.remult.dev/4-concepts/7-access-control/5-sql-relations-filter-for-user-permissions): The sqlRelationsFilter function provides an efficient way to apply row-level filters across related entities directly within SQL, streamlining access control for related rows.
- [Entities as a single source of truth - Field Metadata](https://learn.remult.dev/4-concepts/8-entities-as-a-single-source-of-truth/1-field-meta-data): Entities in Remult are not just a single source of truth for storage, APIs, and authentication—they can serve as a central point for managing any entity-related aspect across your application.
- [Entities as a single source of truth - Forms and Validation](https://learn.remult.dev/4-concepts/8-entities-as-a-single-source-of-truth/2-using-field-metadata-in-forms): This lesson will guide you through creating a form with field-level error handling and dynamic field labels using metadata.
- [Entities as a single source of truth - Lifecycle Hooks](https://learn.remult.dev/4-concepts/8-entities-as-a-single-source-of-truth/3-lifecycle-events): Entity Lifecycle Hooks in Remult provide a powerful way to add custom actions and validations at key stages of an entity’s lifecycle.
- [Entities as a single source of truth - Select Fields](https://learn.remult.dev/4-concepts/8-entities-as-a-single-source-of-truth/4-select-fields): Select fields in Remult allows you to select only the fields you need from an entity.
- [Entities as a single source of truth - Extend Entity (add fields)](https://learn.remult.dev/4-concepts/8-entities-as-a-single-source-of-truth/5-add-fields-extends-entity): When you application grows, you entities will grow too.
- [Testing - Introduction to Testing with Remult](https://learn.remult.dev/4-concepts/9-testing/1-introduction): Remult makes it simple to replace your production database with a test database, enabling you to easily write and execute automated tests.
- [Testing - Testing Api Rules](https://learn.remult.dev/4-concepts/9-testing/2-testing-api-rules): In Remult, automated tests run as if they are executing directly on the backend.

### Examples
- [Validations - Extending Existing Validations](https://learn.remult.dev/9-examples/1-validations/1-extending-existing-validation): In this lesson, you'll learn how to extend and customize existing validations in Remult.

## Agent Skill
- [Remult Skill](https://remult.dev/.well-known/agent-skills/remult/SKILL.md): Install with `npx skills add https://remult.dev` so AI agents (Claude Code, Cursor, ...) know Remult conventions - @Entity, repo(), lifecycle hooks, permissions, ValueList enums, the dual-entity pattern.
